Following modification, IPDASi score increased to 26/35.Twenty subjects (12 pregnancies) participated in fieldtesting;15completedlquestionnaires.Most patiipants89%)woulddefnitlyrecommendthisformofconsutation.Decisional conflict scores decreased $(P<0.001$ ) between T1$(52\pm25)$ and T2$(10\pm16)$ 
coNCLUsloN:Field testing demonstrated thatconsultations using theaid with decision coaching were feasible,reduced decisional conflict and may facilitate shared decision-making.
Journal of Perinatologgy advance online publication, 30 March 2017; doi:10.1038/jp.2017.29

With the prioritizing of patient-centred care, there has been increasing emphasis on patient involvement during decisionmaking processes. Shared decision-making (SDM) involves a collaborative discussion between healthcare professionals and patients to come to an informed,preference-matched decision.Recent statements from national organizations 2,3encourage the use of SDM with decisions at the neonatal limitof viability to enhance parental involvement.

In clinical settings,patientdecisionaidsPtDA）canfacilitate SDM.4They support the participation of patients in decision edge, involvement and values-choice agreement.5,6Decision PtDAandhas similarbenefits.DGTenereiS：4,7,8

When facingthepotentialdeliveryofanextremelmtu infant at23to 24 weeks gestationalage(GA),parents oftenface the choice of having the healthcare team provide either intensive or palliative care for theirinfant. Although evidence strongly supports the use of PtDA and decision coaching to support patients in decision-making,minimal evidence exists regarding their use with parents making a life or death decision for their unborn infant. In 2012,Guillen et $a\bot^{9}$ detailed the systematic development and pre-testing ofa PtDAfor use when prenatally counseling parents facing such a decision. The PtDA was understandable during simulated consultations and increased parental knowledge; however, it lacked standardized 10,11
assessment and was not evaluated in real-life situations.If a PtDA does not meet these certification criteria, it can negatively affect the SDMprocess during clinical use.11

professionals' diffi culties 13with consultations at the limit of viability, the objectives of this study were to:(1) assess and, if necessary, modify an existing $PtDA^{9}$ for use in our institution and (2)field-test decisioncoaching withthePtDAwith parents facing potential delivery at23or24weeksGAto evaluate the feasibility and acceptability of using a PtDA in this situation and to determinetheeffectonparentaldecisionalconflict.
The local ethics review boards approved the study.One author (GM)received permission from its developers to modify an existing PtDA(six decision cards and accompanying fourpage script).

The study was conducted at TheOttawa Hospital, General Campus,and theChildren's HospitalofEasternOntario.Thestudyincluded threemain steps asfollows:)assessment,2)modificationand -testingand)ld (β)testing of decision coaching with the modifiedPtDA.

Four authors(GM,S Ding,S Dunn and TD)performed independent assessments of theexistingPtDAusing theInternational Patient Decision AidStandards instrument （IPDASi,v4.0) that provides a frameworkto certify decision support tools through evaluating and improving their content,development and implementation.Nine of the44IPDASi items mustreceiveyes'forallqualifyingcriteria tobeconsideredaPtDA,a score greaterthanthreeonallcertificationcriteriaforIPDAS certificationand scores >3 on quality items to indicate better PtDA quality. Scoring differences were discussed until consensus was achieved.14Unmet criteria allowed identificationof the existing PtDA's shortcomings.
